summary.p: 0.1-31 (31 lines) INTRODUCTION: Aetiology of the name Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam being given to the city of Campāpati .
0.32-94 (63 lines) SYNOPSIS OF THE ENTIRE POEM BY CANTO.

lg.l: With an appearance that put the the young-rayed (i.e. rising) sun to shame,
a lady with flowing matted locks [and] with a body of shining brightness,
having appeared on the summit of the tall mountain that glitters with/like gold (Mēru)
[she] who had departed towards the south, [she who is] the deity of this island,
having stopped beneath a branching Jaumoon plum tree,
having heard of the sorrow coming to Great Lady Earth,
[she who] had done penance as a harsh enemy to/who had endured the harsh emnity of the demons of harsh vigour
she who is called 'Campu'('Jaumoon Plum'), the Lady of Campāpati ('Town of Jaumoon Plums')–
when there appeared, having mingled with the expanse of foaming water (i.e. the sea)
near to [the town of] Campāpati, whither she had flowed straight towards the east,
the Maiden Kāviri, who had been poured from his pitcher
[Akattiyaṉ] to whom the lovely king/King Kānta/[King] Kāntamaṉ had sacrificed, with desire for the River Gaṅgā,
[Gaṅgā] who exalts the luminuous/sacred family of the red-rayed lord [the Sun].
the ancient female of extreme austerities [Campu], who was sweetly seated there [in the town of Campāpati],
having gladly/joyfully received the maiden of spreading/high-rising/flooding waters [Kāviri] there
with the words, 'O aetherial Gaṅgā, from the affectionate sky!'
Come, o lamp which puts an end to intense desire!'
the man of great penance who doesn't hate (i.e. prefers; or preferred) a back position [Akattiyaṉ], having heard [what had happened] here,
bow down to this ancient lady of extreme austerities [Campu], who is worthy to be (lit. who is of a nature to be) bowed down to by you!'
[And so] the creeper/flag of the Cōḻa family of the undeviating, straight sceptre
–[the Cōḻa family/the sceptre] that has become famous (lit. grown high) in India because of its excellence which is worthy to be sung–
the cool Tamil maiden (Kāviri), who does not change [her] position [i.e. she doesn't dry out],
although, because of the planets changing [their] positions, the summers last longer [there]–
stood still as one who worshipped [Campu]. That very ancient lady [Campu],
having sat, taking [Kāviri] [onto her] lap with overflowing joy,
said, 'On that day on which the ancient one of the red flower [Brahmā] made
the embryos of gods and the embryos of Brahmā
this ancient city of great name took on my name [Campāpati].
[Now] I have given [it] your name (Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam). May you live [long]!'
(1) The announcement [by drum] of the Great Festival
in the beautiful ancient city with a two-part name
to the strong one [Indra] [in the form of] one hundred sacrifices;
(2) and the saying to Mātavi through Vayantamālai
that the gossip had become big;
(3) and the going by Maṇimēkalai inside the flower-grove with its beautiful blossoms in order to pluck big blossoms,
(4) and the entering of the Crystal Chamber, after she had seen in the area
the royal young prince there in that flower-grove;
(5) and that man [the young prince], having seen the girl [Maṇimēkalai] who had entered the Crystal Chamber,
after he had left with grief in his trembling heart;
the coming and manifesting of Maṇimēkalā Deity;
(6) and the carrying off of Maṇimēkalai to Maṇipallavam –
(7) and the rousing of Cutamati from sleep
in the garden by that deity worthy to be spoken of;
(8) and the waking from sleep and the suffering of singular/solitary grief
by the good girl with choice jewels [Maṇimēkalai] there in the interior of that island;
and (9) the realisation of the nature of all her previous births
by her who had suffered through the unique pedestal of shining gems there;
and (10) the giving of a mantra with the words, 'May the anxiety in [your] heart-mind cease!'
by the high goddess [Maṇimēkalā], having appeared before her who had had an epiphany;
and (11) the bestowing on the Innocent Creeper [Maṇimēkalai] a very big vessel,
and (12) the paying obeisance to Aṟavaṇar of great binding penance
by the the Girl with Green-gold Bangles [Maṇimēkalai] together with [her] mothers [i.e. her mother Mātavi and friend Cutamati]
and (13) the narrating in a good way to the Fragrant Flower Garland [Maṇimēkalai],
of the history/origin-story of Āputtiraṉ by His Holiness Aṟavaṇar;
and (14) the manner in which Cintātēvi gave
the vessel to Āputtiraṉ from the palm of her hand;
62 and (15) furthermore the approaching of a big street of that city
by the Innocent Creeper, holding up that vessel for alms;
and (16) the bestowing of alms by the Chaste Lady/Ladies
into the begging bowl of the Bangle-wearing Girl who had received alms
and (17) the approaching of the assembly hall, after curing the insatiable hunger
in the stomach of Kāyacaṇṭikai by the beautiful good girl;
and (18) the going by the king's son [Utayakumaraṉ] [wearing] a garland fragrant with pollen[-producing] flowers
thinking that the Girl with Choice Jewels had approached the assembly hall;
and (19) [Maṇimēkalai] having taken on the form i.e. disguise of the daughter of Vañcaviñcaiyaṉ [Kāyacaṇṭikai]
in front of the royal young prince who had approached the assembly hall –

the manner in which she transformed the sky/heaven/pure-jail-enclosure of the spearman who performed deeds of bravery i.e. violence/warfare [Cōḻa king]/ of the sky-jail-enclosure of the spearman where deeds of bravery i.e. violence/warfare were performed into an enclosure in which dharma is performed;
and (20) the approaching and not withdrawing from the Girl with Choice Jewels
by Viñcaikkāñcaṉaṉ, thinking that [she] was Kāyacaṇṭikai;
and (21) the manner in which Vañcaviñcaiyaṉ kills with [his] strong sword the king's child [Utayakumaraṉ];

and (22) the manner in which she with kohl eyes with red lines of kohl (i.e. the kohl is streaming?) who couldn't bear the sorrow on account of him understood the words (obl. stands for acc here) of the goddess.
and (23) the king with the sounding anklets (Māvaṇkiḷḷi) saying 'Let [the Girl with] Choice Jewels be imprisoned!', and [later] releasing [her];
and (24) the [Girl with] Choice Bangles reaching the land of Āputtiraṉ,
after having talked about good dharma to the [Lady with] the Fragrant Flower Garland [the Cōḻa Queen];
and (25) the setting out to high Maṇipallavam
by the [Girl with] Ornamental Jewels, after she had gone there with him [Āputtiraṉ];
and (26) the manner in which she who had undertaken [this´journey], after taking on the disguise of a man of high penance, reached Vañci with its gold banners;
and (27) her asking religious experts [to tell her] their history,
saying, 'Please tell [me] about the good, flawless meanings!'
and (28) the Flower Creeper entering the great city of Kāñci,
after seeking Aṟavaṇar there with those (i.e.above mentioned) mothers/that mother;
and (29) after she who had entered [the city] had removed the disguise that she had adopted,
furthermore the manner in which she bowed to his [Aṟavaṇar's] feet;
and (30) after adopting the moral practice of penance, and listening to dharma,
the doing of penance by the maiden, saying 'Let the power of sin be cut away!'.
composed, for [the world] to know, in thirty cantos,
the 'Renunication of Maṇimēkalai', through the great, liberal i.e. multifaceted Tamil language.

entry.def: Jaumoon, Malabar, Jāva Plum tree, Syzygium cumini

entry.def: Brahma he who has faces in [all four] directions

entry.def: one who counts/one who is versed in
